graph.confusable_reason: Both produce small, pale-to-yellowish angular/spotty lesions on soybean leaves that fade to brown, and at a casual glance a scout may confuse early bacterial pustule flecks with the chlorotic upper-surface spots of downy mildew. They diverge decisively on the sign present: bacterial pustule forms a raised erumpent pustule on the LOWER leaf surface with NO mold, whereas downy mildew forms a fuzzy gray-to-purple sporulation (fungal growth) on the LOWER surface directly beneath the chlorotic spot. Flipping the leaf and examining the underside resolves them.
graph.shared_presentation: Small foliar lesions on soybean leaflets appearing as pale-green to yellow chlorotic flecks or angular spots, often a few millimeters across, that later turn tan to brown and may coalesce; both occur during the vegetative-to-reproductive canopy, are favored by warm-to-moist conditions, and can cause a peppered or mottled appearance of the upper leaf surface that prompts confusion.

forks.question: Turn the leaflet over and inspect the lower surface directly beneath a lesion: is there a fuzzy mold or a raised bump?
forks.a_signal: A small raised, erumpent PUSTULE (volcano-like bump) at the lesion center on the lower surface, with NO mold, fuzz, or spores; pustule is dry and may rupture.
forks.b_signal: A downy, fuzzy patch of gray, grayish-violet to purple SPORULATION (tufts of fungal growth) covering the lower surface beneath the chlorotic spot, especially in dew/humid mornings.

forks.question: What does the upper-surface spot look like in color and margin?
forks.a_signal: Spots are small angular-to-irregular flecks, often with a reddish-brown center and a narrow yellow halo; lesions tied to a raised pustule.
forks.b_signal: Spots are pale-green to bright-yellow indefinite chlorotic blotches with diffuse margins, later turning brown; no raised center.

forks.question: Under a hand lens, is the lesion center raised or flat?
forks.a_signal: Distinctly raised/erumpent center (the pustule) breaking through the epidermis.
forks.b_signal: Lesion is flat on top; any texture is the mold on the underside, not a raised epidermal pustule.

forks.question: What moisture/temperature conditions preceded the outbreak?
forks.a_signal: Favored by warm temperatures (around 30 C / 86 F) with rain, wind-driven splash, and humid conditions.
forks.b_signal: Favored by cool-to-moderate temperatures with prolonged leaf wetness, heavy dew, and high humidity; sporulation flushes after dewy nights.

forks.question: Are pods or seeds involved, and how?
forks.a_signal: Primarily leaves; severe cases may show small lesions on pods/stems but seed is rarely overtly molded.
forks.b_signal: Can systemically infect pods and seed, leaving a white-to-gray crusty oospore-laden coating on seed coats inside infected pods.

forks.question: How do affected leaves age and senesce?
forks.a_signal: Pustules and flecks remain discrete; severe infection causes ragged tearing/defoliation as lesions dry and drop out.
forks.b_signal: Chlorotic blotches enlarge and merge into large yellow-to-brown areas; heavy infection yellows and prematurely drops leaves with persistent mold underneath.

graph.narrative_a: Bacterial pustule (Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines) produces small chlorotic specks on the upper leaf surface, each with a characteristic minute, raised, erumpent pustule on the LOWER surface at the lesion center. Lesions have reddish-brown centers with narrow yellow halos and remain angular-to-irregular. There is NEVER mold or fuzz, only the dry raised pustule which may later rupture. It is favored by warm, humid, wind-and-rain-splashed conditions. As lesions dry, centers drop out giving leaves a ragged, torn appearance. It is bacterial, spread by splashing water and infested seed/residue, and is the classic look-alike that distinguishes susceptible cultivars (the rxp resistance gene controls it).
graph.narrative_b: Downy mildew (Peronospora manshurica) is an oomycete that produces pale-green to bright-yellow indefinite chlorotic blotches on the upper leaf surface, beneath which a downy, fuzzy gray-to-grayish-violet/purple sporulation develops on the LOWER surface, most visible after dewy, humid nights. The presence of this mold (sporangiophores and sporangia) on the leaf underside is diagnostic. It favors cool-to-moderate temperatures with prolonged leaf wetness and heavy dew. The pathogen can become systemic, infecting young leaves (which may be stunted and uniformly chlorotic) and infecting pods and seeds, leaving a white-to-gray crusty oospore mass on the seed coat. Premature yellowing and leaf drop occur in severe cases.
graph.management_a: Plant resistant cultivars (rxp gene) where available; use clean, certified disease-free seed and rotate away from soybean to reduce infested residue inoculum. Avoid working fields when foliage is wet to limit bacterial spread by splash and contact. Copper-based bactericides give only limited suppression and are rarely economical; cultural controls and resistance are the mainstays. Manage volunteer soybeans and incorporate residue to speed breakdown.
graph.management_b: Use resistant varieties and clean, treated seed since the pathogen is seedborne via oospores on the seed coat; apply a seed-treatment fungicide effective on oomycetes (e.g., metalaxyl/mefenoxam) to reduce systemic seedling infection. Rotate crops and bury infested residue to lower overwintering oospore inoculum. Foliar fungicides are seldom warranted because yield loss is usually minor, but oomycete-active products can be used under prolonged cool, wet, high-humidity conditions in seed-production fields.

lay.farmer_lay_report: My soybean leaves have a scattering of small pale-green to yellow specks and little angular spots, just a few millimeters across, that give the leaves a peppered, mottled look. Some of the older spots are turning tan to brown and starting to run together. It showed up as the plants were bulking up in warm, damp weather.
lay.decisive_lay_a: Flip the leaf over and look at the center of a spot: you'll find a tiny dry, raised bump like a little volcano that can crack open, with no fuzz, mold, or powder on it at all.
lay.decisive_lay_b: Flip the leaf over (best on a dewy or humid morning) and look right under the yellow spot for a downy, fuzzy patch of soft growth that's gray, grayish-purple, or violet, like fine fur.
